edge: The Project Risk Management processes are defined and described in this practice standard, which is based on the PMBOK Guide – Fourth Edition.
Chapter 1 provides an introduction to Plan Risk Management, one of the six processes in Project Risk Management.
Chapter 2 provides a detailed description of Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis, one of the six processes in Project Risk Management.
Chapter 3 provides a detailed description of Plan Risk Responses, one of the six processes in Project Risk Management.

node_1: purpose and objectives of each process
edge: Each chapter in this practice standard addresses the purpose and objectives of each of the six processes in Project Risk Management.
The principles of Project Risk Management are based on constantly evolving tools and techniques.
The principles are the foundation for effective practice of Project Risk Management.

node_2: different approaches to project risk management
edge: Each project requires a different approach to Project Risk Management.
These risk management processes should be tailored to the specific project.
The effectiveness of risk management practices depends on their congruence with organizational culture, processes, and assets.
Project Risk Management can be effectively applied in a global context.
The global context of projects often involves cross-cultural environments.
An organization’s processes can be checked against these principles to validate good Project Risk Management practice.
The Definition of Project Risk Management, as stated in the context, is based on the PMBOK Guide – Fourth Edition.
The objectives of Project Risk Management are to increase the probability and impact of positive events, and decrease the probability and impact of negative events in the project.
Project Risk Management provides a potential return on investment for organizational management.
Project Risk Management should be recognized as a valuable discipline that provides value to project stakeholders (both internal and external).
Project Risk Management is a discipline that affects project management.
Project Risk Management has potential value for team members.
Everyone should be involved in the Project Risk Management process, emphasizing open and honest communication.

node_2: consistent with the value of the project to the organization
edge: Project Risk Management activities should be consistent with the value of the project to the organization.
The level of project risk influences Project Risk Management activities.
The scale of the project influences Project Risk Management activities.
Other organizational constraints may influence Project Risk Management activities.

node_2: subsequent chapters of this practice standard
edge: The execution of the Project Risk Management process is dealt with in subsequent chapters of this practice standard.
Project risk is an uncertain event or condition...

edge: ...that, if it occurs, has a positive or a negative effect on a project’s objectives.
...which can be clearly described.
...also may give rise to uncertainty.

...as well as those which could have a positive effect.

node_2: important to address within a uniﬁed project risk management process
edge: It is important to address both threats and opportunities within a uniﬁed Project Risk Management process.

The term 'Synergies' and 'Efﬁ ciencies' are related in the context as they both refer to benefits gained when multiple tasks or efforts are combined. In this case, they are mentioned together suggesting that achieving synergies can lead to increased efﬁciencies.
The term 'Risks' is defined as uncertain future events or conditions, which implies that risks are a specific type of uncertain future event or condition.

node_2: conditions which would matter if they did occur
edge: The term 'Risks' is defined as uncertain future events or conditions that would matter if they did occur.

node_2: events or circumstances which currently exist or are certain to exist in the future
edge: The term 'Causes' refers to events or circumstances that may give rise to risks, implying they exist now or will exist in the future.
The term 'Effects' refers to conditional future events or conditions that would directly affect one or more project objectives if the associated risk occurs.
When a risk event occurs, it ceases to be uncertain, indicating that an uncertain future event becomes certain when it happens.
Threats which occur are referred to as issues or problems in the context.
Opportunities which occur are referred to as benefits in the context.

node_2: specific events or conditions that might affect project objectives
edge: Individual risks refer to specific events or conditions that can impact the project objectives.

node_2: the combined impact of individual risks on the entire project
edge: Overall project risk refers to the cumulative effect of individual risks on the entire project.
The individual risk may positively or negatively affect the project objectives, elements, or tasks.
Day-to-day Project Risk Management focuses on individual risks in order to enhance the prospects of a successful project outcome.
Overall project risk is more than the sum of individual risks on a project, since it applies to the whole project rather than to individual elements or tasks.
It represents the exposure of stakeholders to the implications of variations in project outcome.

node_2: strategic decision-making, program and portfolio management, and project governance
edge: It is an important component of strategic decision-making, program and portfolio management, and project governance where investments are sanctioned or cancelled and priorities are set.
The risk attitudes of the project stakeholders determine the extent to which an individual risk or overall project risk matters.
The project scale refers to the extent of the project within the scope of stakeholders' overall activities.
Public commitments made about the project refer to promises or guarantees given regarding its performance.
Stakeholders' sensitivity refers to their reaction to issues such as environmental impacts.
Industrial relations refer to the relationships between management and labor in an organization, and stakeholders' sensitivity includes their reaction to such issues.
Stakeholder risk attitudes usually result in a desire for increased certainty in project outcomes.

node_2: preference for one project objective over another
edge: Stakeholders' risk attitudes may express a preference for one project objective over another.
Different organizations are more or less open, and this often impacts how risk is regarded.
Understanding stakeholders’ attitudes toward risk is an important component of risk management planning.

node_2: identifying and managing proactively and deliberately stakeholders' risk attitudes
edge: Identifying and managing proactively and deliberately the risk attitudes of stakeholders is an integral part of the Project Risk Management process.

node_2: may differ from one project to another for the same stakeholders
edge: Stakeholders' risk attitudes may differ from one project to another, even for the same stakeholders.

node_2: will usually differ from one group of stakeholders to another
edge: Stakeholders' risk attitudes will usually differ from one group of stakeholders to another.

node_2: may adopt different risk attitudes at various stages in the same project
edge: A single stakeholder may adopt different risk attitudes at various stages in the same project.
It is important to understand the particular implications of stakeholder risk attitudes on projects where the team is international, cross-industry, or multi-organizational.
The nature of projects is such that circumstances change as they are being planned and executed, and these changes can impact the Project Risk Management.
Risks are uncertainties that might affect the project objectives. As time goes on, the amount of information about these risks increases.
The Project Risk Management processes should be repeated throughout the lifetime of the project as circumstances change during planning and execution.
Some risks will occur while others will not.
New risks may arise or be discovered during the lifetime of the project.
The characteristics of identified risks may change during the project.
The identification and analysis of risks should be revisited periodically to ensure that Project Risk Management remains effective.
The progress on risk response actions should be monitored throughout the project.
If necessary, the action plans should be adjusted accordingly in response to changes in risks or other factors.
The frequency and depth of reviews and updates will depend on the nature of the project and the volatility of the environment in which the project is being implemented.
Successful Project Risk Management relies heavily on communication throughout the process.
The comprehensive input is essential for risk identification and analysis to ensure that all significant risks are not overlooked and are realistically assessed.

node_2: understanding and seen as credible by all concerned
edge: The credibility of the risk management process is ensured only if it is understood and seen as credible by all those who are involved or concerned.

node_2: project risk management process to the rest of the project team
edge: Effective and honest communication from the Project Risk Management process to the rest of the project team is necessary.
The communication of the results should be targeted to meet the specific needs of each stakeholder.
The communication of the results of the Project Risk Management process should be reflected within the overall project communications strategy.
Anyone with an interest in achieving project objectives should play a role in Project Risk Management as risks can affect project objectives.

node_2: common understanding and agreement of the team and other stakeholders
edge: The initiation step ensures a common understanding and agreement of the team and other stakeholders on the approach and parameters that will be applied in managing risk in this project.

node_2: evaluates key characteristics of individual risks enabling them to be prioritized for further action.

node_2: determines appropriate response strategies and actions for each individual risk and for overall project risk, and integrates them into a consolidated project management plan.
edge: Plan Risk Responses is the step that determines the response strategies for individual risks and overall project risk.

node_2: a risk cannot be managed unless it is ﬁ rst identified.
edge: The Identify Risks Process is crucial as a risk cannot be managed without being identified.
The Risk Management Planning has been completed which initiates the first step in the iterative Project Risk Management process.
The purpose of the Project Risk Management process is to identify all the knowable risks to project objectives.
It is impossible to identify all the risks at the outset of a project because some risks are unknowable or may emerge over time.

node_2: iterative nature of identify risks process
edge: The Identify Risks process is iterative, repeating the Identify Risks process to find new risks which have become knowable since the previous iteration.
When a risk is first identified, potential responses may also be identified at the same time.
Where such responses are not implemented immediately, these should be considered during the Plan Risk Responses process.

node_1: critical success factors for the identify risks process
edge: The practices described in these sections will maximize the value and effectiveness of the Identify Risks process and enhance the likelihood of identifying as many risks as practicable.
Early Identification is performed as early as possible in the Project Lifecycle.
Uncertainty is high in the initial stages of a project, so there is often less information on which to base the risk identification.
Early Identification enables key project decisions to take maximum account of risks inherent in the project.
Changes to the Project Strategy may result from Early Identification.

node_2: development and implementation of risk responses
edge: Maximizes the time available for development and implementation of risk responses.
Responses taken early are often normally less costly than later ones, enhancing efficiency.
Risk identiﬁcation is repeated throughout the Project Life Cycle.
The frequency of risk identiﬁcation is determined during the Plan Risk Management process.
Risk identiﬁcation might also be repeated at key milestones in the project.

node_2: significant change to the project or its operating environment
edge: Risk identiﬁcation should be repeated whenever there is significant change to the project or its operating environment.

node_2: identify risks process as defined in the project plan
edge: The Project Risk Management process should permit risks to be identified at any time, not limited to formal risk identiﬁcation events or regular reviews.
The broad range of sources of risk should be considered to ensure that as many uncertainties as possible that might affect objectives have been identified.
The Identify Risks process should ensure opportunities are properly considered.
The Identify Risks process should take input from a broad range of project stakeholders to ensure that all perspectives are represented and considered.
Each identified project risk should relate to at least one project objective (time, cost, quality, scope, etc.)

Risks requiring near term responses may be considered more urgent to address. Indicators of urgency can include the lead time necessary to execute a risk response and the clarity of symptoms and warning signs that may trigger the response.
The risk assessment should be based on agreed-upon Definitions of important terms, and those Definitions should be used consistently when assessing each risk.
The Definitions include definitions of levels of probability, which are used in assessing risks.
The Definitions also include definitions of the impact on objectives, which are used in assessing risks.
The providers of information use the Definitions to give realistic assessments for each risk.
The results of the risk assessment are communicated to management and other stakeholders.

node_2: collection of high-quality information about risks
edge: The Collection of high-quality information about risks is gathered by interviews, workshops, and other means.

node_2: bias should be identified and remedied where possible
edge: When bias occurs, it should be identified and remedied where possible.
A different, unbiased source of information should be found and used when the bias cannot be remedied.

The Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis process adds structure to the list of undifferentiated risks identified in the Identify Risks process.
The information about prioritized risks is usually stored in the risk register as a result of the Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis process.

The Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis process provides a numerical estimate of the overall effect of risk on the objectives of the project based on current plans and information, when considering risks simultaneously. This is done in contrast to the Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis process which adds structure to the list of undifferentiated risks.
The analysis results are used to evaluate the likelihood of achieving project objectives, which is crucial for determining the success of the project.
The analysis helps in estimating contingency reserves appropriate to both risks and risk tolerance of project stakeholders.
Quantitative techniques such as Monte Carlo simulation are used in the analysis for a more realistic estimate of overall project cost or schedule.
A non-probabilistic approach assumes that activity durations or line-item cost estimates are deterministic, which means they are fixed and certain.

node_2: qualitative risk analysis may be appropriate for smaller projects
edge: Qualitative risk analysis may provide enough information for development of effective risk responses, especially for smaller projects.
During the Plan Risk Management process, it is essential to weigh the benefits of quantitative risk analysis against the effort required.

node_2: calculating estimates of overall project risk
edge: The Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis process is focused on calculating estimates of overall project risk.

Partial risk analyses, such as qualitative risk analysis, aim at prioritizing individual risks viewed one at a time and therefore cannot produce measures of overall project risk when all risks are considered simultaneously.
Project risks are usually best understood and quantified at a detailed level such as the line-item cost or schedule activity level.

The implementation of overall risk analysis using quantitative methods requires the assessment of individual risks at the project level of detail to determine the overall impact on the overall project objective.
Project objectives such as achievement of the project’s budget or the schedule are speciﬁed at a higher level, often at the level of the total project.
The results of the quantitative analysis will be compared to the project plan (baseline or current) to give management an estimate of the overall project risk.

node_2: probability of meeting the project’s objectives
edge: The results of the quantitative analysis will give an estimate of the probability of meeting the project’s objectives.
The results of the quantitative analysis provide the organization with the level of certainty it requires based upon its risk tolerance.
Line-item costs are one of the parts of a project.
Schedule activities are one of the parts of a project.
Using quantitative methods helps in estimating overall project risk.

node_2: methodology that correctly derives the overall project risk from the individual risks
edge: The Quantitative Risk Analysis process is based on a methodology that derives the overall project risk from individual risks.

node_1: risk analysis of cost and schedule
edge: An appropriate method for risk analysis of cost and schedule is Monte Carlo simulation.
A decision tree is used for making decisions when future events are not certain.

node_1: probability and impact of all risks
edge: The decision tree uses the probability and impact of all risks to derive an overall project measure.
The risks are specified at the level of the detailed tasks or line-item costs.
The risks are incorporated into the project model to calculate effects on objectives such as schedule or cost.
Attention should be given to the possibility that individual risks in the project model are related to each other.
Several risks may have a common root cause and therefore are likely to occur together.
The possibility of correlated risks is addressed by correlating the risks that are related, ensuring they generally occur together during the analysis.
The risks which occur together can be represented by using the risk register listing of the risk or root cause and attaching it to several project elements.
When a particular risk occurs, the affected elements will all experience the effect of that risk together.
The Quantitative Risk Analysis Process permits representation of many, if not all, of the risks that have impact on an objective simultaneously.
Risk models are used in the Quantitative Risk Analysis Process to represent many, if not all, of the risks that have impact on an objective simultaneously.
The Quantitative Risk Analysis Process permits representation of both opportunities and threats to the project’s objectives.
Quantitative models facilitate the correct calculation of the effect of many risks on the project objectives.
The identified and quantiﬁed risks are typically at a level below the total project.
Probability models use a quantitative method that addresses uncertainty.
The methods should be able to handle the way uncertainty is represented, predominantly as probability of an event’s occurring or as probability distributions for a range of outcomes.

A good example of this is the use of Monte Carlo simulation tools that permit the combination of probability distributions of line-item costs or schedule activity durations, many of which are uncertain.
The 'Data Gathering Tools' include the process of assessing historical data.
The 'Data Gathering Tools' also involve workshops, interviews, or questionnaires to gather quantified information.
The 'Quantitative Analysis Results' can include the probability of a risk occurring.

edge: Performing periodic audits could lead to identification of barriers to effectiveness or keys to success in risk management.

edge: The integrated analysis consolidates the findings of the periodic audits to identify lessons that would be applicable in general to a large proportion of the organization’s projects.

node_1: description of the closure of every risk
edge: The description of the closure of every risk includes the status of each risk (did not occur, occurred and contingency plan invoked, or occurred and impact to the project scope).

node_1: audit of the risk management process

node_1: generally applicable guidelines for the organization
edge: Any generally applicable guidelines for the organization should be highlighted and potential actions proposed for applying them.
The project management plan should include the actions required to monitor and control project risk.
These actions should be set up early in the project planning cycle,
Once risk response planning has been carried out, the agreed-upon actions associated with monitoring specific conditions or metrics should be included in the project schedule.
The agreed-upon, response-related actions should be carried out as a normal part of project execution.

node_1: critical success factors for the monitor and control risks process
edge: Maintaining risk awareness throughout the project is a critical success factor.
The project manager has overall authority over the risk action owner.
Risk management reports should be a regular item on every status meeting agenda.
The senior-level sponsor provides feedback to the project team, which motivates them.

node_1: stakeholders' perception of risk management effectiveness
node_2: way risks are handled as they occur and number or characteristics of such events
edge: Stakeholders’ perception of the effectiveness of risk management is conditioned in part by the way in which risks are handled as they occur, and by the number or characteristics of such events.
The risk action owner collaborates with the risk owner in checking for specific risks that may trigger conditional responses.
All team members remain aware of the importance of risk management due to regular integration into all project management decisions.
When a risk occurs, it is crucial that information about the event and the progress of responses be communicated. This communication is necessary to keep all stakeholders informed.
The effective communication of risk-related information should be supported by a well-executed communications plan.

node_2: project management monitoring and control capabilities
edge: Risk monitoring and controlling involves using standard project management monitoring and control capabilities.
In addition to the standard project management tools, risk monitoring and controlling requires focus on the tools which will support tracking overall risk.
The project manager is responsible for assessing whether the contingency reserves provide the required level of confidence in the success of the project.
Tools for managing time buffers should be closely integrated into the project’s scheduling techniques.
Tools for managing cost should be compatible with the financial practices.
Tools are required to identify trends and forecast future outcomes in order to determine whether the reserves will remain sufficient.
Tools are required for tracking progress and spending in a consolidated manner.
Trigger conditions and the corresponding metrics are defined during the Plan Risk Responses process.
The tools are used to evaluate and track conditions against the project baseline or specified thresholds.
The tools provide information on risks related to the deliverables, such as performance.
The tools can be chosen to provide information on project-related features, such as time and cost.
The tools are required in order to determine, as the project progresses, whether the responses are having the expected effect on the project’s overall level of risk.
A set of quality metrics is used to monitor the quality of the execution of the risk-related plans and processes.

node_2: degree of variation from the baseline
edge: These metrics will normally have been defined in the risk management plan. The degree of variation from the baseline is one of these metrics.
The final control action of risk monitoring and controlling is to record all of the relevant information relating to risk management from start to finish of the project.

The goal is to ensure that the significant risk management information is recorded to provide concrete data to the lessons learned process for inclusion in a lessons learned document, report, or other communication vehicle.

The Risk Management Activities are associated with the Information as they involve providing consolidated information on risks, their occurrence, frequency, effectiveness of actions taken, effort expended, costs and benefits. This information is needed for easy review during the project, at closure, and for future projects.

node_2: guidelines for a pmi practice standard a.1

The focus of the document is consistent with the Project Risk Management section of A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK) which was relevant and generally recognized as good practice on most projects most of the time, and aligns with the concepts and techniques related to Project Risk Management found in this guide.
The charter of the Practice Standard for Project Risk Management was expanded to be consistent and non-contradictory with the PMBOK Guide - Fourth Edition.
The standard was submitted as an exposure draft in the winter of 2007, and exposed on 23 March 2008.
There were 849 comments on the standard submission. The team’s comment acceptance rate was 57%.
The PMI Standards Manager submitted the resultant draft to the PMI Standards Program MAG.
The PMI Standards Program Consensus Body received the draft on 27 March 2009.
Twenty-eight members of the PMI Standards Program Consensus Body agreed to participate in the ballot.

AHP also considers whether they prefer to achieve cost more than time.
AHP also considers whether quality is more important than scope.

The results of AHP are weights that reflect the relative priority of each objective, which sum to 100% and reﬂ ect the relative priority of each objective.
